1.description: Economic interpretation of credit score. Panel A of this figure plots the distribution of credit scores for a representative sample of the FRBNY Consumer Credit Panel/Equifax data in 2005Q1 and the credit scores for our analysis sample of bankruptcy filers six years after filing. Panel B of this figure uses the representative sample from Panel A and plots the observed fraction of individuals with a trade line that is at least 90 days past due (90+ DPD) in 2007Q1 against the observed credit score in 2005Q1. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Credit score trends. This figure plots average credit scores and the implied probability of default for Chapter 13 and Chapter 7 bankruptcy filers. The horizontal axis denotes time, in quarters, relative to the quarter of Chapter 13 flag removal, which occurs seven years after filing. The vertical lines show the quarter of Chapter 13 flag removal and the quarter of Chapter 7 flag removal, which occurs three years later. Outcomes are normalized to the average value of the outcome for Chapter 13 filers in the quarter prior to flag removal. Panel A plots the credit scores for the two groups. Panel B plots the implied probability of default in the next two years. The sample includes Chapter 13 and Chapter 7 filers who were age 30 to 54 at the time of Chapter 13 flag removal, successfully completed the bankruptcy process, had their bankruptcy flag removed sometime between 2002 and 2011, and were not from states that enacted a ban on using credit reports in employment hiring at any time during our sample period. See TableIfor additional details on the outcome measures and sample. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Credit card trends. This figure plots the average credit card outcomes for Chapter 13 and Chapter 7 bankruptcy filers. The horizontal axis denotes time, in quarters, relative to the quarter of Chapter 13 flag removal, which occurs seven years after filing. The vertical lines show the quarter of Chapter 13 flag removal and the quarter of Chapter 7 flag removal, which occurs three years later. Panel A plots the credit card limits for the two groups. Panel B plots the credit card balances. Outcomes are normalized to the average value of the outcome for Chapter 13 filers in the quarter prior to flag removal. The sample includes Chapter 13 and Chapter 7 filers who were age 30 to 54 at the time of Chapter 13 flag removal, successfully completed the bankruptcy process, had their bankruptcy flag removed sometime between 2002 and 2011, and were not from states that enacted a ban on using credit reports in employment hiring at any time during our sample period. See TableIfor additional details on the outcome measures and sample. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Mortgage trends. This figure plots the average mortgage outcomes for Chapter 13 and Chapter 7 bankruptcy filers. The horizontal axis denotes time, in quarters, relative to the quarter of Chapter 13 flag removal, which occurs seven years after filing. The vertical lines show the quarter of Chapter 13 flag removal and the quarter of Chapter 7 flag removal, which occurs three years later. Panel A plots the probability that an individual has at least one mortgage for the two groups. Panel B plots the natural logarithm of mortgage balances plus one. Outcomes are normalized to the average value of the outcome for Chapter 13 filers in the quarter prior to flag removal. The sample includes Chapter 13 and Chapter 7 filers who were age 30 to 54 at the time of Chapter 13 flag removal, successfully completed the bankruptcy process, had their bankruptcy flag removed sometime between 2002 and 2011, and were not from states that enacted a ban on using credit reports in employment hiring at any time during our sample period. See TableIfor additional details on the outcome measures and sample. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Labor market trends. This figure plots the average labor market outcomes for Chapter 13 and Chapter 7 bankruptcy filers. The horizontal axis denotes time, in quarters, relative to the year of Chapter 13 flag removal, which occurs seven years after filing. The dashed vertical lines show the year of Chapter 13 flag removal and the year Chapter 7 flag removal, which occurs three years later. Outcomes are normalized to the average value of the outcome for Chapter 13 filers in the year prior to flag removal. Panel A plots wage employment probability for both groups. Panel B plots the natural logarithm of wage earnings plus one. Panel C plots self-employment probability. Panel D plots the natural logarithm of self-employment earnings plus one. Panel E plots the probability of any employment. Panel F plots the natural logarithm of all earnings plus one. The sample includes Chapter 13 and Chapter 7 filers who were age 30 to 54 at the time of Chapter 13 flag removal, successfully completed the bankruptcy process, had their bankruptcy flag removed sometime between 2002 and 2011, and were not from states that enacted a ban on using credit reports in employment hiring at any time during our sample period. See TableIfor additional details on the outcome measures and sample. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Employment results by gender × race × age × state. This figure plots the distribution of difference-in-differences estimates for employment at three years at the gender × race × age × state level. We weight each estimate by the number of observations. We also show the fraction of estimates that are statistically significant at the 1% level from standard errors clustered at the chapter × cohort × state level. See the text for additional details on the specification and TableIfor additional details on the outcome measures and sample. See Internet Appendix Table XVI for a list of the coefficients. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

7.description: Ex post informativeness of removed bankruptcy flags. These figures plot outcomes for Chapter 13 filers following the removal of their bankruptcy flag, and use nonfilers as a control group for these filers. Observations from states that enacted a ban on using credit reports in employment hiring are included in the figure. Panel A reports the cumulative probability of having a credit card delinquency in the next 16 quarters following the bankruptcy flag removal. These probabilities are estimated by randomly matching a sample of nonfilers to each bankruptcy flag removal year-quarter cohort, and nonparametrically controlling for the flag removal year-quarter interacted with credit score after flag removal. Nonfiler probabilities are reported as the mean for the nonfiler group over time. Bankruptcy filer probabilities are reported as the mean for the nonfiler group plus the estimated coefficient from the previously described regression. Panel B reports results of the same analysis for mortgage delinquencies. Panel C uses a sample of Chapter 13 bankruptcy filers and randomly selected nonfilers who start new jobs the first full year after bankruptcy flag removal. The figure shows the estimated probability of remaining with the same employer, controlling for bankruptcy cohort year fixed effects interacted with industry×wage decile fixed effects. The regressions also control for sex, race, and age. The nonfiler probabilities are reported as the mean for the nonfiler group over time, whereas the bankruptcy filer probabilities are reported as the mean for the nonfiler group plus the estimated coefficient from the previously described regression. Panel D reports results of the same analysis for the overall probability of employment. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
